speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Louis Hofman, H-O-F-M-A-N. This is roll two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hofman, we were talking about the political situation. [INAUDIBLE] What did the Polish people do when they heard that Hitler was coming? What was their attitude toward the Jewish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighbors, they gave us compliments. Hitler is going to come. He's going to kill you. Not all of them, but some of them. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the other ones were thinking. Some of them, they were speaking up. Hitler's going to come on you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do any kind of physical attacks against the Jewish young people? Were there any physical attacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were sometimes incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- for example, on a Saturday, they was all going for a walk. They were attacked by boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened, they had opened up stores against the Jews, Polish people. There was signs-- don't buy by Jews. Buy by your people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in 1939, September 1, when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, every Jew was shaking. We knew what is coming. And something, a [INAUDIBLE], is going to [? cover us up ?] with murder. But you couldn't help it. Everybody was [? hoping, ?] maybe this, maybe that. And then, some of them, they start to go to Russia, because the Bug, the river Bug was six kilometers from my town. And my family didn't move, because we was a very close family together. We couldn't-- only the death sentence could separate us. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the Russians came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans left. The Russians came, and this was another problem. The Russians went back, and the Germans came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939 was a change. There was an agreement between them. The Bug, it's going to be the border, [INAUDIBLE] or something like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did the Russians come?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about three weeks. The Russians came. They was around, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in October already. And then they went back, and the Germans came. Then started [INAUDIBLE] plenty tsuris, plenty trouble. You was going to the Russians, you was getting-- you know, it was a lot of terrible, terrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I could tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them, when they came back, they got arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They go to Bialystok, over the Bug. Whatever was at that time, [? they're ?] [? driven ?] in. And a lot of them, the Russians sent them back, also. When they got them on the border, they sent them back to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did they go to the Russians? Why did the people go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were afraid for the Germans. We know it was the Germans already, what they're doing, what they did to the German Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember where you were when the war broke out, September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: The where? This was on the border from--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where were you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in my town. It was September the 1st, on a Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day? It was on a Friday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday, the 1st of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time? The radio, the Polish radio-- it was a mobilization, soldiers. My brother was raised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him to the army-- Yankev, Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a couple weeks-- a week before, he was called in. And what can I tell you? Right away, when the Germans arrived, is [NON-ENGLISH] For now, the Jews. Everybody get a different [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was still-- everybody had something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened? Did the Germans come into the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in. And then they went back, and the Russians came. Later, a couple weeks, the Russians went back, and the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the Germans returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then started the big trouble for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away everything. They made me to open up the store, and the Polish people came, and they took everything with them, together-- a partnership, a corporation. And they start to give us problems. And you had to pay rent, and you had to pay-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --taxes. To whom did you pay the rent?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Germans. The Germans took over the local government. Then we had to pay taxes and rent and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they soldiers, or were they Volksdeutsche?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Gestapo. It was Volksdeutsche. The Polish people worked for them, for the Germans. They [? watched ?] the law to keep for the Germans, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this organized? How was it organized? Did they open up an office? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized from [? before. ?] The local-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --municipal, and this was existing, but they worked for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the attitude of-- how many Germans did come into this Konstantynow, to your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? At first ?] by a lot of them, but they were [? sitting ?] about 20 Gestapos, I would say so. About 20, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long were they there in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the last minute, when they disappeared. Was changing for ups and downs, came in new ones. They changed the people there, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do? How was the life while they where there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miserable. It was no life. We just existed. It was no life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for how long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Till the end. 'Till the end in 1942, where we went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Konstantynow? What happened with the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: They pushed us in all together in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how this came about, the ghetto, please. And when? Do you remember when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1941. It was ups and downs with the ghettos. They closed off the ghetto, and they opened up the ghetto, and they kept us together. And the worst part of it started when they went [? on the ?] Russians, in June 22, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ghetto were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our town, in [? Konstantynow. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they organize the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a section. In this, we got to stay there. You cannot go in another section.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make a wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fence around.

speaker: interviewer
text: A fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] from the wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: From wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people did they have in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Already was about 1,500 less, because a lot of them died already. A lot of them had been out to Russia. Maybe about 1,200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The living condition was terrible-- four, five people in one room, six people. Three families in a one bedroom, and a living room and a kitchen. Three families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1940 to '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between 1940 and '42, were you in the Konstantynow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Together with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and my whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after they took away your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I didn't go there. I didn't have any-- it was no store anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of [NON-ENGLISH]. It's hard to explain, with what we was living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you have food? From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to organize at night, go out to get something, to buy something, to give away a suit and get potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To whom did you give away this suit?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Polish people, to the neighbors. You picked up the good ones. The other ones, the bad ones, they left you right away, and they brought you to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How else did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very-- when it comes to food, it's very hard to say, because we didn't have food. It was tsuris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad. We couldn't get medical supplies. People was dying right and left. There was no doctor there. It was at the Polish side. They wouldn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jewish doctors, were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ther was never a Jewish doctor in my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the hygienic conditions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: tsuris-- very bad. Typhus, sickness-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know any people that died of typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. A lot of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you guarded in the ghetto? Were there any guards? Were there [NON-ENGLISH] in the ghetto? The SS? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were you guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The SS. [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, they was not far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. They was watching. They kept an eye on this. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many SS watching the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was this SS. It was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have-- was this only the 20, or were there more?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. There was maybe more. They was in and out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear an armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everyone has to wear an armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an armband did you have to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a white strip about four inches wide, and a Magen David from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --write down on it, or sewn on it, an armband. Did anybody try to run away from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, they were running away from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to run away, in the time, at night, when they used to come to get the people to slave labor. They'd send them out to Majdanek, to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --Sobibor and so forth. I used to run away at night, jump out, and I went in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this that they were sending? Tell me about this situation. How was this organized, that they were sending people out from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad. What do you mean about organization?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this come about? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They took-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just to destroy the Jewish people. They took them to labor camp, some far away, and they never come back. They was killed there by the Germans, by the Polish watchmen, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in the ghetto, did they take you out for hard labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I escaped a lot of times. I used to jump out, run out through the window. The houses were smaller houses. One family had, like, a-- and I used to jump out of it and end up through the window, and I went out from the city-- a mile, two miles, whatever. After they left, then I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they register the inhabitants of Konstantynow, or did they know how many people are living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a list. They was asking to give them a list, how many people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --lives in that town. This was in the beginning. They gave them a list, the rabbi with a [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] --from the [INAUDIBLE], from the Jewish organization, and they took the list and brought them there, 18 miles to Biala Podlaska. There was the residents from the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave them a list of all the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they come to count the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they make the people go on to the different camps? How did this come--

speaker: interviewee
text: They went and they came at night, and they went from house to house. There was a man. They took them out. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, with Ukrainians, with Polish. With their helpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they did this at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did this at night, and then in the daytime, in the morning, 6, 7 o'clock, 8 o'clock, they took them to the-- they had to go 20 miles, walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used no cars, no trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, sometimes, was a truck. But sometimes they had to-- they went by foot. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone of your family was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, was my two brother-in-laws, they took them. And they sent them away to Belzec. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They was there a few weeks, and they came back. Somehow they sneaked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it already general knowledge, where people were being taken? How did the people know where they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know where they're taking them. But when they came to the place, where they stopped already to arrive, that they know where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did the people who remained in the town know where the other people were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, we sent a Polish man after them to find out where-- where they took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then, when they came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few came back. The other ones, they got killed there-- hard labor, no food. The labor was unbelievable-- bad. They're digging-- digging ditches. They got killed there, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take anything with them from home, like clothes and packages, money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They took [INAUDIBLE], so they didn't take nothing with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the meantime? Between 1940 and '42, what was happening with your parents and your family? Tell me about your father and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- he took sick. We couldn't take in a doctor. We couldn't get medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He passed away. He's [INAUDIBLE] man, but he passed away, normal. The normal time, he would be alive, if we had a doctor or medicine. But that time was nothing, no doctor, no medicine, and he died six days before Purim, 1942. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Six days before Purim. What was he sick with?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had pneumonia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother-- Tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, when we was already out-- they sent us out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have to come later to it, because my mother-- we went from in our town-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --a day-- after Yom Kippur, 1942. There was an order-- no, I'm mixing up. The 6th of June, 1942, was an order from the capital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like every one Jew has to come into Biala Podlaska. There is going to be a Judenstaat. And we was thinking, this is the biggest lie in the world. The Germans, they know how to frame up these things. We start to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Sarnaki. It was a different district. To [INAUDIBLE], we went in the woods. Just a very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What woods? What was the name of the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the town, was all kind of names in the forest. Was terrible to go away, to send the people to that. We sent it with Polish people, with horse and wagon. At night, we used to sneak out from the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing a lot for my family, and another family and another family. We start to run away from that town, because otherwise-- this was on a Sunday. Till Friday, we had to be there in Biala Podlaska. There is going to be the Judenstaat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you send the people away?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [? Lowicz, ?] Sarnaki, and in the woods, in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you organize this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We organized. We took farmers with the horse and wagon this way. We tell them to go not in the main roads, only from the side streets, from the side roads. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take-- did the people whom you sent away, did they take some of their possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We paid them in cash for the trip. They picked up the best people, the best people-- we could trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people take some of their possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The farmers, we picked up the best one, but we had [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't took away. They took them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they just go with the clothes on their back? They didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they took something with them. But what happened, that particular day, they didn't arrive to Biala. They took 3,000 people, Jewish people. They sent them to Sobibor. How did they know? They sent a Polish man to track where they're going. And he came, and with the answer, and they went to Sobibor. And there was already a crematorium and gas chambers, and they finished them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the man who gave you this news, the Polish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he direct didn't give me. He gave it to the Biala. It was 18 kilometers, but we find it out a day or two later. If we would go there, we would be finished on June the 6th, 1942, after Shavuot. Was a couple days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of going to Biala as the order was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: You escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: We escaped. And we tried--

speaker: interviewer
text: You took your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was there. It was spreaded, one in [? Lowicz, ?] one in Sarnaki, one in the woods. I was the only one near the town to know the news, what's going on. In that particular [? week ?] was the older people. They couldn't go no place, and they didn't know where to go. They didn't have help. They dressed up themselves in Yom Kippur, [NON-ENGLISH]. But Friday came a message. The Germans were not going to send them out anymore to the Russians. But this was not [INAUDIBLE]. They're supposed to go to Russia, but they went to Sobibor. They're not going to do anything more. They could come back. And they started coming back. We had so much tsuris to coming back-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? What kind of tsuris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone was attacking the Jews to come back. Everyone-- a Polish, and the Russian prisoners. Everyone was attacking.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they were beating them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not beating. They used to take away the [INAUDIBLE] from them, and a sofa. It was very hard to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, change it. Tell me, how is my expression?
